channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: How a Chemical Tank in Southern California Came to the Brink of Disaster
item.description: Firefighters are left without any options to prevent the tank, filled with a toxic chemical, from collapsing or exploding. The authorities have urged thousands of people to evacuate their homes.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:credit: Caroline Brehman/Reuters
item.media:description: A blocked-off road in Garden Grove, Calif., on Friday.

item.title: Garden Grove residents rush to safety, with no idea when they can go home.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Fairley Raney
item.media:credit: Rebecca Raney for The New York Times
item.media:description: Evacuees went to a recreation center in Garden Grove on Friday.

item.title: HUD Moves to Limit Assistance Animals for Disabled Tenants
item.description: Housing officials were told to exclude emotional support animals and tighten the definition of a service animal when granting accommodations for disabled tenants.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron and Debra Kamin
item.media:credit: Jason Kempin/Getty Images
item.media:description: A service dog at the 2023 Veterans Day parade in Nashville.

item.title: Garden Grove Is a Suburban City Near Theme Parks
item.description: The city is home to part of Little Saigon, the cultural heart of the Vietnamese American population in California.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff
item.media:credit: Jae C. Hong/Associated Press
item.media:description: Garden Grove has a large Vietnamese American population and is known for sharing Little Saigon with the neighboring city of Westminster.

item.title: Trump Weighs His Options in Carrying Out New Strikes in Iran
item.description: There is no shortage of targets if he decides to strike: Energy facilities left untouched, the deep underground nuclear storage site at Isfahan and missile sites that appear to have been dug out.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger, Eric Schmitt, Tyler Pager, Jonathan Swan and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s announcement on Friday that he was skipping the wedding this weekend of his son Donald Trump Jr. came as negotiations with Iran over its nuclear program and its blockage of the Strait of Hormuz appeared to have hit a dead end.

item.title: What is GKN Aerospace, the Company Whose California Plant Is at Risk of Exploding?
item.description: A chemical leak occurred at one of the company’s manufacturing plants in California, raising concerns about an explosion.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra and Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Allen J. Schaben
item.media:description: Water is sprayed on a chemical tank at GKN Aerospace.

item.title: What Is Methyl Methacrylate, the Chemical at the Center of the Garden Grove Crisis?
item.description: The E.P.A. warns that short-term exposure to the compound can cause skin and eye irritation and respiratory issues.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn
item.media:credit: Alessia Pierdomenico/Bloom, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Methyl methacrylate is widely used to manufacture resins and acrylic plastics. It can be found in products like advertising signs, light fixtures, plumbing and latex paint.

item.title: Meth and Fentanyl Found in Home Where Emergency Responders Fell Ill
item.description: More than a dozen emergency workers were treated after responding to a report of an overdose at a New Mexico home.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker
item.media:credit: Savannah Peters/Associated Press
item.media:description: The authorities in New Mexico identified the substances that killed three people and hospitalized more than a dozen emergency responders on Wednesday.

item.title: U.S. to Block Entry to More Noncitizens Who May Have Been Exposed to Ebola
item.description: The Trump administration announced plans to expand a ban on entering the United States to legal permanent residents who had been in the Democratic Republic of Congo, Uganda or South Sudan.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Hamed Aleaziz
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health care workers check temperatures as people pass through a checkpoint in Goma, Democratic Republic of Congo, on Thursday.

item.title: Lawsuit Argues Trump’s $1.8 Billion Fund Excludes Those He Targeted
item.description: The constellation of individuals and groups involved claims to have suffered partisan attacks by the federal government under Trump, yet would not be compensated.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rioters during the attack at the Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021.

item.title: A Ford Truck, Home to Newborn Robins, Is Stuck at a Kansas Car Dealer
item.description: Employees of a dealership in Olathe, Kan., found the nest, which is protected under federal law, on top of one of the truck’s tires.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.category: Sports Utility Vehicles and Light Trucks

item.title: In Dissecting 2024 Loss, Democrats’ Report Ignores Gaza, Biden’s Age
item.description: The nearly 200-page draft autopsy indicated that the party had failed to fully grapple with why Kamala Harris had lost to Donald J. Trump.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kamala Harris speaks at the Democratic National Convention in Chicago in 2024.

item.title: White House Approves $9 Billion for Spy Agencies to Catch Up on A.I.
item.description: The C.I.A. and N.S.A. cannot fully deploy the latest models on their classified systems because of a shortage of cutting-edge chips.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz and Julian E. Barnes
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jensen Huang, president and CEO of NVIDIA, gives a presentation in Washington last October.

item.title: The D.N.C. Chairman, Trump’s $1.8 Billion Fund and More Politics News
item.description: He has focused on internal politics, building a loyal base despite tactical disputes.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Jenn Ackerman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Martin has fulfilled a campaign promise to dispense more funds to each of the D.N.C.’s state parties.

item.title: Trump Administration Offers Conflicting View of Civil Rights Cuts
item.description: Education Secretary Linda McMahon has said she wants to hire more civil rights lawyers, but the White House has proposed cutting that office in half.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: In March of last year, Ms. McMahon fired about half of her department’s civil rights office. In December, she gave up on the firings in the face of mounting legal challenges and an expanding backlog of discrimination complaints in schools.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.description: Evacuation orders were issued to residents in parts of Orange County after a chemical leak that was initially reported on Thursday grew more serious.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra

item.title: Maryland Democrats Plan to Eliminate State’s Lone Republican Seat in Time for 2028
item.description: A top state lawmaker who opposed drawing new districts for the fall election now says he supports a constitutional amendment that could pave the way to an all-Democrat map for the next cycle.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Bryan Woolston/Associated Press
item.media:description: Bill Ferguson, left, the Democratic leader of the Maryland Senate, has been at odds with Gov. Wes Moore, a Democrat, over whether to redraw the state’s congressional boundaries this year.

item.title: G.O.P. Break With Trump Reflects Limits of Party’s Post-Jan. 6 Truce
item.description: Republicans, seeing President Trump’s personal agenda diverging from their political interests, vented their outrage about paying those who threatened their lives on Jan. 6, 2021.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Bill Cassidy, Republican of Louisiana, at the Capitol this week after losing his primary to a Trump-backed challenger.

item.title: Green Card Seekers Must Leave U.S. to Apply, Trump Administration Says
item.description: The change is likely to affect hundreds of thousands of people. It could also lead to more family separations as spouses or relatives wait for application decisions, immigration lawyers said.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo and Albert Sun
item.media:credit: Libby March for The New York Times
item.media:description: There are various pathways for foreigners to obtain green cards, which grant them the ability to live and work in the United States as permanent residents.

item.title: Waymo Suspends Service in Six Cities After Cars Drove Into Flooded Roads
item.description: The temporary shutdowns came after videos emerged showing two Waymo cars stopped on swamped streets in Atlanta on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Waymo also announced it was temporarily halting its highway service this week to improve its navigation system.

item.title: Judge Dismisses Criminal Case Against Kilmar Armando Abrego Garcia
item.description: The move deals an embarrassing blow to the Trump administration, which made the Maryland man, Kilmar Armando Abrego Garcia, the face of its deportation campaign.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Kilmar Armando Abrego Garcia outside a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement in Baltimore last year.

item.title: Five members of Trump’s cabinet have stepped down.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Cai and Lily Boyce

item.title: Biden Won’t Endorse Becerra, or Anyone Else, in California Governor Race
item.description: Former President Joseph R. Biden Jr. is not planning to endorse his health and human services secretary, Xavier Becerra, or any other candidates in the race.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Godofredo A. Vásquez
item.media:description: Xavier Becerra, a Democratic candidate for California governor, served as health and human services secretary under President Joseph R. Biden Jr.

item.title: Tulsi Gabbard Resigns as Director of National Intelligence. Trump Names Aaron Lukas Acting Chief.
item.description: Ms. Gabbard had a difficult tenure in the Trump administration and was seldom seen in the room when the president made important national-security moves.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz and Julian E. Barnes
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Tulsi Gabbard at a House hearing in March. She said on Friday that she would remain in her post as the nation’s intelligence chief until June 30.

item.title: Kitty Bruce, Caretaker of Lenny Bruce’s Legacy, Dies at 70
item.description: As Mr. Bruce’s daughter, she maintained boxes of video and audio of his groundbreaking and often scandalous work.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: American Doctor With Ebola Says He Is ‘Optimistic’ for Recovery
item.description: Dr. Peter Stafford, a medical missionary in the Democratic Republic of Congo, tested positive for Ebola on Sunday. He is receiving treatment in Germany.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: via Serge
item.media:description: Dr. Peter Stafford being transported to Germany for medical treatment for Ebola.

item.title: Lutnick Donated $5 Million to House Republicans Before Epstein Testimony
item.description: Howard Lutnick, the commerce secretary, made the donation after agreeing to an interview with lawmakers about his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer and Ana Swanson
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Howard Lutnick, the secretary of commerce, at a hearing in Washington last month.

item.title: Wasserman Schultz Will Run in Majority-Black District in South Florida
item.description: Republicans drew the former D.N.C. chairwoman out of her district. Her decision to run in Florida’s 20th Congressional District, however, has stoked tensions with Black Democrats.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Debbie Wasserman Schultz, a Democrat, will run in a majority-Black district in South Florida after Republicans drew her out of her own district.

item.title: Fact-Checking the Trump Administration’s Inaccurate Claims About the $1.8 Billion Fund
item.description: The president, vice president and acting attorney general have offered a series of inaccurate claims to defend an unusual fund announced this week.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu and Jamie Leventhal
item.media:credit: Carolyn Van Houten for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has suggested the $1.8 billion fund is not particularly unusual, but critics say it essentially establishes a way to funnel taxpayer money to Mr. Trump’s allies.

item.title: Why This Ebola Outbreak Could Be One of the Worst
item.description: An Ebola outbreak is spreading in parts of Africa with over 170 deaths and about 750 infections, according to the World Health Organization. Our global health reporter Apoorva Mandavilli explains how the suspension of vital channels of U.S. aid is turning a bad situation much worse.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli, Alexandra Ostasiewicz, Stephanie Swart, Rafaela Balster, Nikolay Nikolov and Luke Piotrowski

item.title: Partisan Mud Fight, or Focus on the Midterms? Redistricting Divides Democrats.
item.description: Gov. Abigail Spanberger of Virginia urged her fellow Democrats, including the House minority leader, Hakeem Jeffries, to stop talking about drawing new congressional lines for partisan advantage.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein, Lisa Lerer and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times, Pool photo by Mike Kropf
item.media:description: Representative Hakeem Jeffries of New York and Gov. Abigail Spanberger of Virginia.

item.title: Kevin Warsh is Sworn in as Fed Chair Against Backdrop of Rising Inflation Threat
item.description: The economic backdrop that Kevin M. Warsh inherits as chair of the Federal Reserve does not call for the interest rate cuts that President Trump wants.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh was sworn in as Federal Reserve chair during a ceremony with President Trump on Friday at the White House.

item.title: In Idaho’s Suburbs, a Rat Invasion Tests the Limits of Small Government
item.description: Irrigation canals around Boise have served as a rat superhighway, bringing an infestation so serious that health officials have floated declaring a public emergency.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Aldo Guerrero, a pest technician, looks toward a rat bait station set next to a home by a canal in Boise, Idaho.
